topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The doorbell rang, a sharp, insistent sound that grated on my nerves. I snatched the heavy package off the mat, my jaw tight. It was addressed to the previous occupant, a reminder of a lingering administrative chore. I tore open the flimsy box with unnecessary force, the sound of crinkling paper echoing in the quiet apartment. Inside, there was a stack of outdated utility bills and a single, dusty ceramic mug. "Honestly," I muttered to the empty hallway, shoving the items onto a nearby shelf with unnecessary aggression, "can't they just send the actual mail?" I paced back and forth, my foot tapping a restless rhythm against the hardwood floor, waiting for the next thing to demand my attention.

[story 2]

My eyes scanned the delivery notification on my phone, and a slow, simmering frustration began to bubble in my chest. Another box. Another item left for me to deal with. I dragged the parcel from the entryway, the cardboard rough beneath my fingertips. It was clearly meant for the unit above, a ghost from a past tenancy. I threw it onto the kitchen counter with a decisive thud, the sound jarring in the stillness. I leaned against the counter, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, my gaze fixed on the door, anticipating the moment someone would finally show up to resolve this lingering inconvenience.

[story 3]

I stared at the package sitting on the welcome mat, my knuckles white where I gripped the edges of the box. It was a pointless delivery, an unnecessary piece of mail from someone long gone. I let out a slow, audible sigh, the air escaping my lungs in a puff of exasperation. I didn't bother with the tape; I just ripped it open, the contents spilling out—old photographs and a dried-up flower. "Just get it over with," I whispered, the words laced with a sharp edge. I immediately started shuffling the contents, not caring about sorting them, just needing the task to conclude so I could move on to something more engaging.
